original_abstract: Chlorophylls are essential dietary antioxidants found in green vegetables. In this study, we investigated the specific mechanisms by which the $a$ and $b$ forms of chlorophyll and pheophytin (its magnesium-dechelated derivative) reduce oxidative stress. Using the comet assay, we demonstrated that these compounds protect human lymphocytes from DNA damage induced by hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$). We also evaluated their chemical antioxidant capacity through DPPH radical scavenging and their ability to chelate ferrous iron [Fe(II)], a key catalyst in the formation of reactive oxygen species. Furthermore, we measured their effectiveness in preventing lipid peroxidation by monitoring thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) during copper-mediated oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Our results revealed that all tested chlorophylls and pheophytins exhibit significant, dose-dependent antioxidant activity, with the pheophytins emerging as the most potent variants. These findings suggest that chlorophyll-related compounds guard against oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation through a dual mechanism: the direct scavenging of free radicals and the chelation of pro-oxidant metal ions.
humanized_abstract: Like biliverdin and bilin, chlorophylls are dietary antioxidants that can be found in a number of green vegetables. The purpose of this study was to assess the roles and importance of the various members of this class, specifically the a and b forms of chlorophyll, and pheophytin, their magnesium-dechelated derivative. Using the comet assay technique we show that each chlorophyll derivative significantly protects human lymphocytes from hydrogen peroxide-induced DNA damage. Antioxidant activities in terms of their ability to scavenge DPPH radicals and their capacity to chelate the pro-oxidant ferrous iron [Fe(II)] are shown. We also determined the inhibitory effects on the production of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) generated from copper-mediated oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Generally, each of the various forms of chlorophyll and pheophytin demonstrated dose-dependent effects towards being a more powerful antioxidant, with a clear trend towards the pheophytins. Consequently, our studies confirm the effects of chlorophyll derivatives towards oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation via a dual mechanistic role in scavenging free radicals and chelating pro-oxidative transition metal ions.

We also determined the inhibitory effects on the production of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) generated from copper-mediated oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Generally, each of the various forms of chlorophyll and pheophytin demonstrated dose-dependent effects towards being a more powerful antioxidant, with a clear trend towards the pheophytins. Consequently, our studies confirm the effects of chlorophyll derivatives towards oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation via a dual mechanistic role in scavenging free radicals and chelating pro-oxidative transition metal ions.
